Name: kernel_optimization_for_llm_inference
Title: Empirical Kernel Optimization for Efficient Inference of Large Language Models
Short Hypothesis: Investigate the impact of kernel fusion and memory bandwidth optimization on the latency and throughput of LLM inference, focusing on empirical measurements rather than theoretical assumptions.
Related Work: Existing literature emphasizes various optimization strategies for LLM inference, such as mixed precision (e.g., DiffPro) and dynamic scheduling (e.g., AdaFuse). However, these works often lack a detailed, empirical kernel-level analysis that evaluates specific CUDA/Triton kernel modifications and their direct impact on hardware performance metrics. Our approach aims to fill this gap by focusing on reproducible benchmarks and profiling to identify precise hardware bottlenecks.
Abstract: This proposal seeks to advance the efficiency of large language model (LLM) inference by focusing on kernel optimization through empirical profiling and measurement. We will conduct a series of experiments to evaluate the impact of kernel fusion, memory bandwidth utilization, and cache layout on inference latency and throughput. By analyzing kernel performance metrics such as occupancy, achieved memory bandwidth, and warp stall reasons, we aim to identify specific optimizations that lead to measurable improvements in real-world deployments. Our methodology prioritizes reproducibility, utilizing fixed hardware configurations and precise benchmarking techniques to ensure that results are generalizable and actionable. This research will provide insights into effective kernel optimization strategies that can be directly translated into practical improvements for LLM deployments, thereby bridging the gap between theoretical optimizations and real-world performance.
Experiments: Conduct A/B tests comparing baseline LLM inference with standard CUDA implementations against optimized fused kernels designed for specific operations (e.g., attention and feed-forward layers).
Profile the performance of each configuration using nvprof/Nsight to collect latency, throughput (tokens/sec), and memory bandwidth utilization data.
Test various KV-cache layouts to determine their impact on memory access patterns and overall latency.
Perform a sensitivity analysis on tile sizes and kernel parameters to identify the most effective configurations for reducing wall-clock time while maintaining accuracy.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential risks include the variability in performance results across different hardware configurations, which may limit generalizability. Additionally, while focusing on kernel optimizations, some improvements may be hardware-specific and not applicable to all deployment scenarios. The complexity of accurately measuring and attributing performance gains to specific optimizations may also pose challenges in ensuring reproducibility.
